**On-boarding for project “Benchmarking preprocessing of imaging-based spatial transcriptomics”**

**Introductory lecture. Date:** 

1) What is spatial data: technologies, why spatial data, challenges (Valentina)  
2) Intro to txsim: the goal, methods, evaluation (Louis)  
   

Link to the presentation: [link](https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/14A-iK13WLqKcj_ewbsVk0rchdJ6GnB6NiEBOe3GlMA4/edit?usp=sharing)

**Journal Club. Date:** 

1 presentation per method/student \- 15-20 minutes  
   
Presentation structure:

1) Introduction: what does the method/tool do? Why do we need it?   
2) Method description: the main principle of work/algorithm   
1) What does the method take as an input? What kind of data preprocessing is demanded?   
2) Hyperparameters of the method: what are they responsible for? How to select them/what are reasonable ranges to test them?  
3) Output of the method

     3\)  Output evaluation  
     4\)  Benefits of the method in comparison of similar methods 

Please, put more emphasis on the method section. 

**Start work. Date:** 

1) Choose the method that you want to implement. It’s better to take the method that you discussed at journal club.   
2) Check if you have access to the github repository: [link to repo](https://github.com/openproblems-bio/task_ist_preprocessing). If not, contact Louis.   
3) Install viash: [https://openproblems.bio/documentation/fundamentals/requirements](https://openproblems.bio/documentation/fundamentals/requirements) . Let Valentina know if you have any issues with installation. For advanced work you also need to install Docker [https://docs.docker.com/engine/install/](https://docs.docker.com/engine/install/)   
4) Clone the repo: 

\# Setting up the git repo  
git clone https://github.com/openproblems-bio/task\_ist\_preprocessing.git  
git submodule init  
git submodule update  
\# or in one:  
git clone \--recurse-submodules https://github.com/openproblems-bio/task\_ist\_preprocessing.git

5) Create a new branch at task\_ist\_preprocessing repo from **dev** branch. From time to time (especially before you create another new branch from dev) update your branch with command `git fetch`  or `git pull` (which corresponds to `git fetch` \+ `git merge`)  
6) For the new method create a new conda environment. Install all necessary packages for your method. At the end you will save the working environment with all installed packages as a yaml file.    
   

**Test data**  
If you successfully installed Docker on your laptop, download the data with   
bash ./scripts/sync\_resources.sh  
Or you can get data directly from Valentina. Put them in the folder resources\_test

 **Method work**  
   
You will work with: [Annotated data object](https://anndata.readthedocs.io/en/latest/) , [scanpy](https://scanpy.readthedocs.io/en/stable/tutorials/basics/clustering.html) 

1) To properly run a method you should know what method takes as an input. Look at the requirements: should these be raw counts, normalized counts, log-normalized counts, scaled? Check if your dataset satisfies the requirements.   
2) Run the method with default parameters. What is the method output? Are there any evaluation methods provided?   
3) Explore the output with different hyperparameters. If there are too many of them, choose the most important.   
4) What are the biologically relevant ranges of the hyperparameters values? Or is there a way to infer the best value from the data? 

**Integrating the method into the txsim with viash:**  
   
You will work with: [SpatialData package](https://spatialdata.scverse.org/en/stable/tutorials/notebooks/notebooks.html) (minimum knowledge: SpatialData object structure ), viash

1) Firstly, check if viash is installed at your computer, and if it works properly: 

   

   viash \--help 

   

2) Your method corresponds to one component in viash: [https://viash.io/guide/component/create-component.html](https://viash.io/guide/component/create-component.html).

Example of the simplest component in txsim:

task\_ist\_preprocessing/src/methods\_segmentation/custom\_segmentation/

Question for self-check:

1) What is the function of each part of viash config? (e.g. name: The name of the component, engines: Lists which engines a component can target \- Native or Docker, etc)  
2) What does executable do?   
3) Structure of the script  
4) The most basic steps that happen when you run the viash component?   
5) What are native and docker engines?  
   

Now try to run one ready component: 

\# Always execute from the base directory  
cd task\_ist\_preprocessing

\# Convert all components in the repo into exectuables (located in ./target)  
viash ns build

target/executable/segmentation\_methods/custom\_segmentation/custom\_segmentation \---engine native \--input resources\_test/common/2023\_10x\_mouse\_brain\_xenium\_rep1/dataset.zarr \--labels\_key rep1\_cell \--output tmp/label\_image.zarr

target/executable/assignment\_methods/basic/basic \---engine native \--input resources\_test/common/2023\_10x\_mouse\_brain\_xenium/dataset.zarr \--transcripts\_key rep1\_transcripts \--segmentation\_input tmp/label\_image.zarr \--coordinate\_system rep1\_global \--output tmp/assigned\_transcripts.zarr

Explanation:  
path to your executable: target/executable/segmentation\_methods/custom\_segmentation/custom\_segmentation  
It can be docker or native \---engine native  
Path to input file \--input resources\_test/common/2023\_10x\_mouse\_brain\_xenium\_rep1/dataset.zarr   
Parameter for method running \--labels\_key rep1\_cell  
Path where to send the output \--output tmp/label\_image.zarr

Check if now you have a folder tmp with a file label\_image.zarr and assigned\_transcripts.zarr. 

Check the result of the methods work in separate ipynb file:   
Don’t forget to install spatialdata package 

import spatialdata as sd  
import spatialdata\_plot

sdata \= sd.read\_zarr("../task\_preprocessing\_imagingbased\_st/resources\_test/common/2023\_10x\_mouse\_brain\_xenium/dataset.zarr")  
sdata\_transcripts \= sd.read\_zarr("../task\_preprocessing\_imagingbased\_st/tmp/assigned\_transcripts.zarr")

sdata.points\["transcripts"\] \= sdata\_transcripts\["transcripts"\]

crop0 \= lambda x: sd.bounding\_box\_query(  
    x,  
    min\_coordinate=\[10000, 10000\],  
    max\_coordinate=\[10300, 10300\],  
    axes=("x", "y"),  
    target\_coordinate\_system="rep1\_global",  
)

\# Not sure why, but these tables prevented cropping from working  
del sdata.tables\["metadata"\]  
del sdata.tables\["rep1\_cell\_table"\]

sdata\_crop \= crop0(sdata)

sdata\_crop\["transcripts"\] \= sdata\_crop\["transcripts"\].astype({'cell\_id': 'category'})

sdata\_crop.pl.render\_labels("rep1\_cell").pl.render\_points(  
    "transcripts",color="cell\_id",  
).pl.show(coordinate\_systems="rep1\_global", figsize=(10, 5)) 

Question to check yourself: 

1) How would you interpret the output (plot)?  
2) What methods were run in this component?   
   

**Add your method**

1) You have to be in your branch  
2) Add a new folder to the corresponding method. For example, if you add segmentation method:  task\_ist\_preprocessing/src/segmentation\_methods/\<name\_of\_segmentation\_method\>

3) copy paste config.vsh.yaml , script.py from task\_ist\_preprocessing/src/segmentation\_methods/custom  into your new folder  
4) Wrap your method in your script.py. The simplest example is in custom. More advanced example of the segmentation method (Cellpose):

![][image1]

Lines 35-41:  The part between VIASH START and END will be completely ignored when viash runs the script. The only aim of this block is for development purposes. The parameters in par should be valid files/parameters for quick copy pasting when testing the script.  
Lines 43-47: processing of the values of parameters from config.vsh.yaml. For example, converting ‘None’ from string into None.  
Line 49-50: Get image for cellpose image segmentation method   
Line 51: Necessary for image segmentation method. If you work with segmentation method, please, read about [transformations](https://spatialdata.scverse.org/en/stable/tutorials/notebooks/notebooks/examples/transformations.html). For any details contact Valentina.  
Line 53: Output of the component will be in the format of spatialdata object.   
Line 54: Converting image into numpy array (relevant for segmentation methods)  
Line 55: Necessary for image segmentation method. If you work with segmentation method, please, read about [transformations](https://spatialdata.scverse.org/en/stable/tutorials/notebooks/notebooks/examples/transformations.html). For any details contact Valentina.  
Line 56: Run your method (don’t forget to import it at the beginning of your script. In this code it was done at the Line 1 import txsim as tx)  
Line 57: conversion of the method output to lower dtype (ignore it for your code. But if you work with segmentation method, this may be useful. Contact to Valentina, she will send the code for convert\_to\_lower\_dtype function)  
Line 58-60: conversion of the method output to Spatialdata object ([additional information is here](https://spatialdata.scverse.org/en/stable/tutorials/notebooks/notebooks/examples/models2.html))  
Line 63-65: save the output

5) arguments need to be listed in config.vsh.yaml (you will use them with par\['\<arg\_name\>'\]) (Attention\!\!\! Make sure that your parameters are listed as it’s implied in viash  language: [https://viash.io/reference/config/arguments/](https://viash.io/reference/config/arguments/) . For example, “float” values are “double” in viash with respect to “type”)  
   ![][image2]  
6) 

\# Convert all components in the repo into exectuables (located in ./target)  
viash ns build \-q component\_name

\# Run an executable in your current (conda) env. NOTE: only works if native engine was added in the config.vsh.yml

./target/path/to/executable \---engine native \--script\_arg1 ... \--script\_arg2 …

Example:

target/executable/segmentation\_methods/binning/binning \---engine native \--input resources\_test/task\_ist\_preprocessing/mouse\_brain\_combined/raw\_ist.zarr/ \--output tmp/outputbinning.zarr

**Results**  
After running the component, please, find the output files. For the example above it would be the tmp/outputbinning.zarr. Depending on your method (segmentation, labels assignment, etc) the post-processing and analysis of the output is different. In our meetings we will discuss how you should visualise the output for your specific method.   
Example for one of the segmentation methods:

import spatialdata as sd  
import spatialdata\_plot

sdata \= sd.read\_zarr("../task\_preprocessing\_imagingbased\_st/resources\_test/common/2023\_10x\_mouse\_brain\_xenium/dataset.zarr")1  
sdata\_transcripts \= sd.read\_zarr("../task\_preprocessing\_imagingbased\_st/tmp/assigned\_transcripts.zarr")

sdata.points\["transcripts"\] \= sdata\_transcripts\["transcripts"\]

crop0 \= lambda x: sd.bounding\_box\_query(  
    x,  
    min\_coordinate=\[10000, 10000\],  
    max\_coordinate=\[10300, 10300\],  
    axes=("x", "y"),  
    target\_coordinate\_system="rep1\_global",  
)

\# Not sure why, but these tables prevented cropping from working  
del sdata.tables\["metadata"\]  
del sdata.tables\["rep1\_cell\_table"\]

sdata\_crop \= crop0(sdata)

sdata\_crop\["transcripts"\] \= sdata\_crop\["transcripts"\].astype({'cell\_id': 'category'})

sdata\_crop.pl.render\_labels("rep1\_cell").pl.render\_points(  
    "transcripts",color="cell\_id",  
).pl.show(coordinate\_systems="rep1\_global", figsize=(10, 5))

![][image3]

**Reproducibility**

The second important step after running and implementation of the method is its reproducibility. This means that another user would be able to run your method from the start to the end without any issues. For that you need to have:

1) Clean environment that includes all dependencies with resolved installations conflicts  (if they exists)  
2) Absence of all local paths and variables

For that you need to check your environment. Save your clean working environment as a yaml file. Create a new empty env. Then install all dependencies from the saved yaml file.   
If there are no conflicts, run your code (where you prepared the method for integration to the viash pipeline) and then the entire component.   
   
At the end create a README.txt file and describe the issues that you faced.   
Examples of the issues:

What are specific methods requirements?  
Are there any installation issues?   
Potential preprocessing requirements of the data for method running: normalization? Log10? Scaling?   
Are there any memory issues?   
How long does it take for you to run the method with the given test data (minutes, hours, days…)?  
What is the output of the method?   
Any other issues

**Test your method**

Testing helps you to debug your code and control the run of the final pipeline: [https://viash.io/guide/component/unit-testing.html](https://viash.io/guide/component/unit-testing.html) 

For test properly running tests your config file needs to include  the line \_\_merge\_\_: /src/api/comp\_method\_segmentation.yaml  
(DEPENDS ON YOUR METHOD: comp\_method\_segmentation is appropriate for segmentation method)  
You can find this line here: \- custom segmentation script [https://github.com/openproblems-bio/task\_ist\_preprocessing/blob/main/src/methods\_segmentation/custom\_segmentation/config.vsh.yaml](https://github.com/openproblems-bio/task_ist_preprocessing/blob/main/src/methods_segmentation/custom_segmentation/config.vsh.yaml)  
 

Script [https://github.com/openproblems-bio/task\_ist\_preprocessing/blob/main/src/api/comp\_method\_segmentation.yaml](https://github.com/openproblems-bio/task_ist_preprocessing/blob/main/src/api/comp_method_segmentation.yaml)  has the lines:

test\_resources:  
  \- path: /resources\_test/task\_ist\_preprocessing/mouse\_brain\_combined  
    dest: resources\_test/task\_ist\_preprocessing/mouse\_brain\_combined  
  \- type: python\_script  
    path: /common/component\_tests/run\_and\_check\_output.py  
  \- type: python\_script  
    path: /common/component\_tests/check\_config.py

The command that allows you to run the code-test: 

viash test path/to/config.vsh.yaml

Final steps:  
After you successfully run the test, create a pull request in github and assign it Louis and Valentina.
